coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis and characterization of AgNPs/CDs hybrid system for enhanced oxidation resistance at physiological concentration of H2O2
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Carbone, Giorgio Giuseppe
Manno, Daniela
Serra, Antonio
Buccolieri, Alessandro
Cennamo, Sabrina
Aziz, Muhammad Rizwan
Gabriele, Alessandra
Calcagnile, Lucio
coredata.dc:description: Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) have emerged as promising antimicrobial agents due to their unique properties and broad-spectrum antimicrobial action. However, their interaction with hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in the bloodstream can lead to their oxidation, thereby reducing their antimicrobial effectiveness and potentially posing threats to human health. This study presents a novel approach to enhance the oxidation resistance of AgNPs by integrating them with carbon dots (CDs) to form an AgNPs/CDs hybrid system. CDs, with their exceptional biocompatibility and functionalized surface, can interact with AgNPs to provide a protective layer. Our green synthesis method employed microwave-assisted techniques using only three reagents: silver nitrate, l-tryptophan, and glucose. The characterization of the hybrid system was conducted using Ultraviolet–Visible spectroscopy (UV/Vis), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), High Resolution TEM (HRTEM), and Finite Element Analysis (FEA). UV/Vis spectroscopy revealed a red shift in the absorption peak of the hybrid system compared to pure AgNPs, confirming the interaction between CDs and AgNPs. TEM and HRTEM analyses, supported by the PASAD plug-in and Image Pro‐Plus software, respectively, elucidated the structural arrangement and interaction of these nanoparticles. Additionally, FEA computations using FreeFEM software platform forecasted the resonant wavelengths.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs)
Carbon dots (CDs)
Hybrid system
Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2)
HR-TEM
Oxidation resistance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical characterization of CDs/AgNPs hybrid system
Structural characterization of CDs/AgNPs hybrid system

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the Biopen Lab of the \u201CE. De Giorgi\u201D mathematics and physics department of the University of Salento for the use of the Jeol JEM-ARM 200F Holo-TEM.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Above figure depicts the streamlined synthesis protocol: Sol-A, formed by combining 625 µL of 10–2 M AgNO3 with 25 mL of 10 % glucose solution, is heated in a microwave at 600 W for 8 min. Concurrently, Carbon Dots (CDs) are synthesized from glucose and l-tryptophan (6:1 ratio) to create Sol-B. Post-heating, Sol-B is added to Sol-A, stirred continuously for 15 min to form the AgNPs/CDs mixture (Sol-C).

Above figure shows the interaction mechanisms in the CDs/AgNPs hybrid system. The formation of the system involves mixing CDs and AgNPs solutions, leading to van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonding. Van der Waals forces arise from temporary dipoles in the electron clouds of the particles, contributing to the hybrid system's stability. Hydrogen bonds, involving a hydrogen atom bridging two electronegative atoms, further stabilize the system, akin to interactions observed in CDs and AgNPs.

(a) Absorption spectrum of silver nanoparticles without carbon dots (CDs), demonstrating the baseline optical properties without the influence of CDs. UV–Vis spectrum of AgNPs/CDs (black) and AgNPs alone (red). (b)Schematic and absorption spectra of the AgNPs/CDs hybrid system, showing both simulated and experimental results that validate the interaction between silver nanoparticles and carbon dots.

(a) Bright field image of the of AgNPs/CDs solution and related SAED pattern in the inset both acquired with the Hitachi 7700. (b) Histogram of the nanoparticles size dispersion. (c) Derived diffraction profile with PASAD plug-in from SAED pattern shown in the inset of Fig. 4a.

(a) High resolution image of a AgNPs/CDs hybrid system nanoparticle. (b) FFT of the nanoparticle shown in Fig. 5a (c) Contribution of the Ag (1 1 1) isolated with Image-Pro Plus from the FFT shown in Fig. 5b. (c) Contribution of the C (1 0 0) isolated with Image-Pro Plus from the FFT shown in Fig. 5b. (e) Composite image obtained merging Fig. 5a (by colouring it green), 5c and 5d

(a) An image of a larger AgNPs/Cds hybrid nanoparticle. (b) The FFT of the nanoparticle shown in Fig. 6a. (c) Composite image obtained merging Fig. 6a (coloured in green) and the contribution of CDs isolated by Fig. 6d (coloured in red).
Absorbance variation over time for AgNPs/CDs (black line) and AgNPs (red line).
(a), (b), (c) TEM Figures of the fresh AgNPs (S-1), AgNPs + 10–6 H2O2 (S-2) and AgNPs/CDs + 10–6 H2O2 (S-3) solutions. (d), (e), (f) TEM SAED patterns of the figure (a), (b), (c) respectively.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis and characterization of AgNPs/CDs hybrid system for enhanced oxidation resistance at physiological concentration of H2O2

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mathematics and Physics “E. De Giorgi”, University of Salento, 73100, Lecce, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mathematics and Physics “E. De Giorgi”

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mathematics and Physics “E. De Giorgi”, University of Salento, 73100, Lecce, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Applied Physics, Dating and Diagnostics (CEDAD), Department of Mathematics and Physics “E. De Giorgi”, University of Salento, 73100, Lecce, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mathematics and Physics “E. De Giorgi”

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Applied Physics, Dating and Diagnostics (CEDAD), Department of Mathematics and Physics “E. De Giorgi”, University of Salento, 73100, Lecce, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) have emerged as promising antimicrobial agents due to their unique properties and broad-spectrum antimicrobial action. However, their interaction with hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in the bloodstream can lead to their oxidation, thereby reducing their antimicrobial effectiveness and potentially posing threats to human health. This study presents a novel approach to enhance the oxidation resistance of AgNPs by integrating them with carbon dots (CDs) to form an AgNPs/CDs hybrid system. CDs, with their exceptional biocompatibility and functionalized surface, can interact with AgNPs to provide a protective layer. Our green synthesis method employed microwave-assisted techniques using only three reagents: silver nitrate, l-tryptophan, and glucose. The characterization of the hybrid system was conducted using Ultraviolet–Visible spectroscopy (UV/Vis), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), High Resolution TEM (HRTEM), and Finite Element Analysis (FEA). UV/Vis spectroscopy revealed a red shift in the absorption peak of the hybrid system compared to pure AgNPs, confirming the interaction between CDs and AgNPs. TEM and HRTEM analyses, supported by the PASAD plug-in and Image Pro‐Plus software, respectively, elucidated the structural arrangement and interaction of these nanoparticles. Additionally, FEA computations using FreeFEM software platform forecasted the resonant wavelengths.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, the excessive and unregulated use of antibacterial agents in both clinical and agro-zootechnical settings has led to the development and spread of a wide range of bacterial strains characterised by antibiotic resistance [1,2]. This dynamic poses a serious threat to public health, as the ability of antibiotics to fight bacterial infections is fundamental to modern medicine. In order to counter this growing threat, the scientific community is intensifying research into the development of new antimicrobial compounds [3,4].

In this regard, the use of nanotechnology is proving particularly promising. Materials such as metal nanoparticles [5], metal oxide nanoparticles [6], carbon nanomaterials [7] and their composites currently represent a viable alternative to tackle the challenge of antibiotic resistance.
Among these nanoparticles, silver ones (AgNPs) stand out due to their remarkable antibacterial, antifungal and antiviral properties, combined with high biocompatibility [8,9].

Traditional methods of synthesising AgNPs often involve the use of toxic chemicals, high temperatures and processes that can be harmful to the environment and human health [10]. Consequently, there is a growing interest in a green chemistry approach [11].

This synthetic method uses reducing agents and stabilisers of natural origin, such as plant extracts [12,13], microorganisms [12] and polysaccharides [13]. In this way, biocompatible AgNPs can be produced, which is crucial for applications in the medical field.

However, AgNPs show some limitations when introduced into biological environments, such as the bloodstream. In these environments, they can interact with reactive oxygen species (ROS), including hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) [14].

H2O2 is naturally produced in human body as part of various metabolic processes. For example, certain enzymes, such as NADPH oxidases [15], can produce H2O2 through the transfer of electrons from oxygen to hydrogen ions. These enzymes are mainly found in cells of the immune system that use H2O2 as a defence against pathogens [16,17]. Mitochondria also produce a significant amount of H2O2 through cellular respiration [18].

In general, all cells produce H2O2 from multiple sources, but through a process called ROS homeostasis [19], the intracellular concentration of H2O2 does not exceed 10 nM [20]. The removal of H2O2 in the blood is not as efficient. According to Forman et al., the concentration of hydrogen peroxide under physiological conditions is 1–5 μM and can be as high as 50 μM in inflammatory diseases [21].

At these concentrations, silver nanoparticles can be oxidised, reducing their antimicrobial properties, and potentially causing harm to the human body [22,23]. One possible cause of the AgNPs' toxicity in humans is their dissolution into silver ions (Ag+) because of interactions with oxidizing agents such as H2O2 [24]. For example, Zaheer et al. demonstrated how Ag+ ions can interact with proteins and alter their functionality, thus posing a health risk [25].

Recent studies have explored various approaches to improve the oxidation resistance of AgNPs in biological contexts. Cunningham et al. designed a series of AgNPs stable to surface oxidation and Ag+ ions release by coating them with a hybrid lipid membrane [26]. Guzmán-Soto et al. covalently bound human serum albumin protein to the surface of lipoic acid-coated AgNPs generating an oxidation-resistant hybrid system [27]. Jin et al. successfully synthesised AgNPs that resist oxidation thanks to a capping consisting of graphene oxide [28].
To improve the oxidation resistance of AgNPs, in this article, we decided to use carbon dots (CDs) to create a hybrid AgNPs/CDs system.

CDs are carbon nanoparticles with dimensions between 2 and 10 nanometres. They exhibit excellent optical and electronic properties, making them promising for a wide range of applications [29–32]. As shown by Gedda et al., CDs exhibit significant free radical scavenging property (EC50: 13.87 μg mL-1), total antioxidant activity (EC50: 38.72 μg mL-1) and excellent antimicrobial activity [33]. Because of above mentioned reasons, we have chosen to study the CDs/AgNPs hybrid system for the present work.

CDs are also characterised by surfaces functionalised with carboxyl and hydroxyl groups that can interact with AgNPs [34,35], forming hybrid systems. Another reason to work on the AgNPs/CDs system is because of its good biocompatibility [23,36]. In the literature, it's been reported that this combination exhibits a synergistic effect, where the properties of both silver nanoparticles and carbon dots are optimized. For example, Wang et al. created an AgNPs/CDs hybrid system that showed excellent synergistic antibacterial performance against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria [37]. Moreover, When AgNPs are combined with carbon dots, a type of carbon-based nanomaterial known for its low toxicity [38–40] and high biocompatibility [41,42], the resulting hybrid system demonstrates a significantly improved biocompatibility. For example, Ma et al. studied the cytotoxicity of a CD-AgNPs hybrid system on Bcap 37 cells. The cell viability values were above 90 %, demonstrating the low cytotoxicity of CD-AgNPs towards cells [43]. Therefore, the hybrid system of silver nanoparticles and carbon dots emerges as a more biocompatible alternative not only for its applications as antimicrobial agent but also expanding its potential usage in areas like drug delivery [44], bioimaging [45,46], antibacterials [47], and biosensing [48–50].

In the present work, the green synthesis of CD-modified AgNPs/CDs via a microwave-assisted method was performed using only three reagents: silver nitrate, l-tryptophan and glucose. The main objective of this work was to test whether CDs were able to protect AgNPs from oxidation through an in vitro test in the presence of H2O2 at physiological concentrations.

A second objective was to provide experimental proof of the interaction between CDs and AgNPs, using optical spectroscopy and HRTEM in combination with FFT. As reported by Arroyave et al., this aspect has been studied a little but is of vital importance for identifying possible applications of the AgNPs/CDs hybrid system [51].

Silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.5%) was purchased from Fluka Analytical®, while glucose monohydrate (C6H12O6 · H2O, 99.0%) and l-tryptophan (C11H12N2O2, 99.0%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich®. Deionized water was obtained by a Zener Up 900 (Human Corporation®) water purification system and used throughout the experiments.

In a typical synthesis, 625 µL of a 10–2 M aqueous solution of AgNO3 was added to 25 mL of a 10 % glucose solution (Sol-A). An aqueous solution of C-Dots was obtained through a microwave assisted hydrothermal synthesis using glucose and l-tryptophan as precursors in a 6:1 ratio (Sol B).

To obtain the AgNPs/CDs solution (Sol-C), Sol-A was heated in a microwave at 600 W for 8 min and a few drops of Sol-B were added to it before it cooled down, keeping the solution in constant stirring for 15 min. A representation of the synthesis protocol is shown in Fig. 1 .

Ultraviolet–Visible spectroscopy . A UV/Vis spectrophotometer model T80 (Pg Instruments Ltd) was employed to study a) the optical characteristics of the AgNPs/CDs colloidal solution by recording its absorbance in the range between 300 and 800 nm; b) the response to H2O2 in physiological concentration by monitoring the absorbance variation with time.

The optical features of the synthesised AgNPs/CDs hybrid system were assessed through finite element analysis (FEA) to forecast the resonant wavelengths. The FEA computations were executed utilizing the FreeFEM software platform [52].

Transmission Electron microscopy (TEM) . Conventional TEM images and corresponding electron diffraction patterns were acquired utilizing a Hitachi® 7700 transmission electron microscope, operated at an accelerating voltage of 100 kV. Additionally, High Resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were recorded using a Jeol® JEM-ARM 200F NEOARM, operating at 200 kV. Specimens for TEM analysis were prepared by drop-casting freshly synthesized solutions containing silver nanoparticles conjugated with carbon dots (AgNPs/CDs) onto a standard carbon-supported 600-mesh copper grid. This was followed by a slow natural air-drying process.

Electron diffraction patterns were analysed within the Gatan® Digital Micrograph software package, utilizing the PASAD plug-in [53]. The processing of TEM images was conducted instead using Image Pro‐Plus software while the structural characteristics of the AgNPs/CDs hybrid system were elucidated through Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) analysis on HRTEM images.
Before providing a hypothesis on the formation of the AgNPs/CDs hybrid system, it is appropriate to clarify the mechanisms leading to the formation of the AgNPs and CDs used in this article.

The formation of AgNPs exploits a green method in which glucose, used as a reducing agent, is combined with silver nitrate using microwave energy. According to previous studies, the microwave rapidly provides the energy required for glucose to reduce silver ions (Ag+) and form AgNPs [54–58].

The synthesis of CDs was also assisted by microwaves, using glucose and tryptophan as precursors. The complete reaction mechanisms involved in this synthesis are not yet fully understood, but we believe that the precursors undergo thermal degradation due to the influence of microwaves.

Glucose, for example, is a simple sugar composed of six carbon atoms, twelve hydrogen atoms and six oxygen atoms. When exposed to high temperatures, it undergoes dehydration (caramelisation) and forms reactive intermediates such as 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) [59,60]. The intermediates formed during the thermal degradation of glucose and tryptophan are highly reactive and can interact with each other to form the CDs [61,62].

In order to obtain a CDs/AgNPs hybrid system the resulting CDs and AgNPs solutions were mixed. According to the literature, as shown in Fig. 2 we assume that the formation of a CDs/AgNPs hybrid system is due to the formation of van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonds between AgNPs and CDs.

Van der Waals forces, also known as dispersion forces, are weak intermolecular forces present among all types of molecules. When CDs and AgNPs approach each other, the electron cloud around each particle begins to fluctuate, creating temporary dipoles, among which van der Waals forces are generated, resulting in the formation of a stable CDs/AgNPs hybrid system [63].

Hydrogen bonding is another important intermolecular force that plays a crucial role in the formation of the CDs/AgNPs hybrid system. Hydrogen bonding is a special type of intermolecular force that is established between a hydrogen atom covalently bonded to a highly electronegative atom and another highly electronegative atom. The hydrogen atom acts as a bridge between the two electronegative atoms, creating a strong attractive force between the two atoms. Jiménez-López et al. indicated in their study that the interaction between AgNPs and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) may be due to the formation of hydrogen bonds between the carboxyl groups of GQDs and the surface of AgNPs [64].
The CDs synthesised in this work also have carboxyl groups on their surface, so it is reasonable to assume that the formation of the CDs/AgNPs hybrid system may be due to these interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization of CDs/AgNPs hybrid system
ce:section.ce:para: UV–Vis spectrophotometry was used to study the optical characteristics of the CDs/AgNPs solution. In Fig. 3 a two absorption peaks are clearly visible, the one at 280 nm which is attributable to the π-π* transition of the sp2-hybridised carbon atoms of the CDs [65] and the one in the spectral range 350–500 nm with a maximum at 420 nm related to the LSPR of the AgNPs [66].

Compared to silver nanoparticles without CDs, whose spectrum is shown in Fig. 3a, the peak of the Ag/CDs hybrid system is shifted towards the red. It is reasonable to assume that this shift is due to the interaction of the carbon nanoparticles with the silver ones. In fact, it is known that the electric field associated with the LSPR acts as a transducer, converting changes in the local refractive index into a frequency shift of the LSPR peak [67].

The optical characteristics of silver nanoparticles decorated with carbon dots were simulated using a finite element method (FEM) to predict the resonance wavelengths. The FEM calculation was done using FreeFEM software [52].

In the numerical model, both X and Y directions employed a periodic boundary condition with infinite periodicity. For the Z direction, the boundary condition was modeled as a perfectly matched layer to represent an infinite space. Within the model, the nanostructures are positioned in a square lattice, maintaining an edge-to-edge spacing of 30 nm. Central to this setup is a silver sphere with a 10 nm diameter, surrounded by 6 carbon nanoparticles. Each of these carbon particles has a diameter of 2.5 nm and they are distributed along the X, Y, and Z axes of the primary silver nanoparticles.
This is illustrated in a schematic diagram provided in the inset of Fig. 3b, which displays the simulated absorption spectra when the direction of propagation is along the X axis.

Fig. 3b also shows the absorption spectra, both simulated and experimental, of the AgNPs/CDs hybrid system. The coherence between these results offers substantial validation for the interaction between silver nanoparticles and carbon dots, corroborating the theoretical framework and the experimental observations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of CDs/AgNPs hybrid system
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 a presents a typical overview of nanoparticles in bright-field mode. The characteristic Z contrast allows the nanoparticles to be clearly discriminated from the supporting carbon film. More details on the nanoparticle size dispersion (diameter d in nm) are provided by the histogram, illustrated in Fig. 4b. We would like to point out that the TEM images were processed using Image Pro-Plus software. In particular, the nanoparticle size distribution was ascertained through a series of digitised TEM images acquired from 50 randomly selected fields.

In the inset of Fig. 4a, the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern corresponding to the previously discussed image area is depicted. The continuous rings observed in the diffraction pattern are in line with the expected behaviour of nanoparticles with random orientations, reflecting the face-centred cubic lattice (fcc) inherent to metallic Ag. Taking advantage of the capabilities of the PASAD tools software, the diffraction pattern was subjected to an in-depth analysis. The software was used to identify the center of the acquired SAED pattern. After identification, the elliptical distortions were rectified with sub-pixel resolution. Subsequently, the software transformed the SAED patterns into electron diffraction profiles via azimuthal integration. The derived profile is presented in Fig. 4c. By fitting this profile with PASAD, two salient contributions are clearly visible: one associated with Ag and the other with carbon dots. These resulting peaks were systematically attributed and catalogued as shown in the table in Fig. 4.

It is important to point out that to maintain methodological precision, the software independently located the spline points, supervising the initial phase of peak detection and fitting. This comprehensive approach ensured a methodical and consistent analysis of the diffraction patterns, which allowed the intricate structural attributes of nanoparticles to be elucidated.

In differentiating between silver and carbon nanoparticles the employment of Z-contrast proved to be inadequate. To overcome this limitation high-resolution analysis of the nanoparticles was performed.

All the observed AgNPs/CDs nanoparticles in HR-TEM mode, including the smallest ones, exhibit a defective and irregular lattice, as exemplified in Fig. 5 a. These distortions, clearly explained by the FFT conducted on the nanoparticle, are characterised by an atypical spacing and orientation with respect to the lattice planes of a uniform material (e.g. only silver). Consequently, two crystalline reflections can be distinguished, corresponding to the lattice planes Ag (1 1 1) and C (1 0 0), as shown in Fig. 5b.

Subsequently, the Image-Pro Plus software was used to distinguish the lattice plane contributions of Ag (1 1 1) and C (1 0 0) more clearly. Starting from the FFT, it was possible through Image-Pro Plus to effectively isolate the contributions of silver and carbon, which are shown in Figs. 5c and 5d respectively. Finally, we realised a composite representation to delineate the distinct localizations of the Ag and CDs nanoparticles within the hybrid system. In Fig. 5e, the entire nanoparticle is shown in green. Superimposed on the entire nanoparticle are the characteristic lattice planes of Ag (1 1 1) and C (1 0 0), shown in blue and red, respectively. The resulting composite image serves as an integrative visualisation, effectively capturing the spatial orientation and distribution of the nanoparticles in relation to the identified lattice planes.

The same approach was used to analyse larger nanoparticles, as in the example shown in Fig. 6 a. The corresponding FFT, shown in Fig. 6b shows a much more complex and disordered situation in which crystalline reflections from Ag and C are simultaneously present. The circles drawn on the FFT (appropriately marked) identify the distance from the center at which the different reflections are located, summarized in the table at the top right of Fig. 6.

Again, the contributions of the CDs were isolated in the FFT, using the same procedure as mentioned above, to obtain an image in which the lattice fringes of the CDs appear in red. Subsequently, this image was juxtaposed with the green image of the entire nanoparticle to obtain the image shown in Fig. 6c. From the analysis of Fig. 6c, it is evident that the AgNPs have incorporated the CDs, thus giving rise to hybrid nanoparticles, whose structure is fully compatible with the modelling that allows the optical absorption curve to be fitted and interpreted.

In the current study, we investigated the reactivity of the AgNPs/CDs composite and a AgNPs solution without CDs when exposed to physiological concentrations of H2O2. Both experiments were executed under identical conditions. A volume of 2 mL from each nanoparticle solution was introduced into distinct cuvettes. This was followed by the addition of 250 µL of a 10–6 M H2O2 solution to each cuvette. The reactivity towards H2O2 was assessed by tracking the absorbance at one-minute intervals over a 20-minute period. The acquired absorbance values were normalized against the initial absorbance measured before the H2O2 addition. The comparative results are presented in Fig. 7 .

In general, from a chemical point of view, the interaction of AgNPs with a strong oxidising agent, such as H2O2, can induce their oxidation. It has been reported that H2O2 undergoes a Fenton-like reaction with AgNP producing the highly reactive OH˙ and causing oxidative dissolution of AgNP into Ag+ [68]. A critical aspect of this interaction is the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), predominantly hydroxyl radicals. These highly reactive species interact with AgNPs, possibly intensifying the oxidation effects and influencing the stability and reactivity of the nanoparticles. The interaction between H2O2 and AgNPs impacts the surface structure and crystallinity of the nanoparticles. The formation of Ag+ ions and the activity of ROS lead to a rearrangement of atoms on the nanoparticle surface, often accompanied by a reduction in crystallinity. This is evidenced by TEM and SAED analysis and is discussed in the following paragraphs.
This phenomenon, as shown in Fig. 7, occurs through a progressive decrease in absorbance over time.

At cellular and molecular level, as confirmed by some studies such as the one conducted by Levard et al. [69], this oxidation reaction leads to the formation of silver oxide (Ag2O) according to the redox reaction:
(1) 2H2O2 (l) + 2Ag (s) → Ag2O (s) + 2H2O (l)

Silver oxide (Ag2O) can dissolve in aqueous solutions (like human blood), releasing Ag+ ions which can interact with proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids, compromising their functions and inducing oxidative stress and cellular inflammation [70].

Instead, in the research presented here, the AgNPs/CDs hybrid system proved capable of resisting the oxidising action of hydrogen peroxide. As shown in Fig. 7, the absorbance of the AgNPs/CDs hybrid system remained constant over time, which is indicative of the ability of CDs to prevent the oxidation of AgNPs.

Finally, we performed a series of experiments to verify the protective effect of CDs against the oxidation of AgNPs in the presence of H2O2 in the AgNPs/CDs hybrid system. We hypothesized that AgNPs would oxidize into silver ions, leading to some modification in the morphology of the particles. To verify this, we examined two samples under TEM, labeled S-1 and S-2.

S-1 served as a reference and contained only the AgNPs solution while S-2 represents AgNPs solution with the addition of 250 μL of a 10–6 M H2O2 solution. A comparison of the TEM images from both samples (Fig. 8 a and 8b respectively) revealed significant changes. In the presence of H2O2, the AgNPs in S-2 had partly shifted from circular shapes to non-circular ones with rough surfaces. This indicates an oxidation process induced by hydrogen peroxide, where surface silver atoms are oxidized, altering the crystal structure.

The oxidation of AgNPs upon interacting with H2O2 was further confirmed by examining the SAED patterns of each sample. The SAED pattern from S-1, shown in Fig. 8d, displays a high degree of crystallinity in the AgNPs. However, the SAED patterns from S-2, seen in Fig. 8b, reveal a noticeable loss in crystallinity.

We conducted a similar test on an AgNPs/Carbon Dots (CDs) hybrid system, referred to as S-3. After introducing the same H2O2 amount as before, we observed that the particle shape in S-3 remained unchanged, as evident in Fig. 8c. Moreover, the AgNPs preserved their crystallinity as evinced by the SAED pattern shown in Fig. 8f. This result indicates the antioxidative properties of the Carbon Dots, which protect the AgNPs from oxidation at the tested concentration of H2O2.
This study examined the formation and optical and structural properties of an AgNPs/CDs hybrid system synthesised using a green synthesis method.

From the optical point of view, the spectral characteristics of the AgNPs/CDs hybrid system show a clear red shift compared to the peak of a classical AgNPs solution, suggesting an interaction between the carbon and silver particles. This interaction was further supported by the results of simulations using the finite element method (FEM).

Structural characterisation revealed the orientation and distribution of the nanoparticles in the hybrid system. Using HR-TEM analysis, it was revealed that the AgNPs/CDs nanoparticles exhibit a defective and irregular lattice structure, suggesting a fusion between the carbon and silver nanoparticles.

A significant observation concerns the reactivity of the AgNPs/CDs hybrid system in the presence of H2O2. While the interaction of AgNPs with H2O2 can induce their oxidation, the hybrid system showed surprising resistance to oxidation, suggesting the potential ability of CDs to protect AgNPs from oxidation. This observation has significant implications for the use of nanoparticles in biological environments, where oxidation can lead to the generation of reactive oxygen species and potential toxic effects.

In summary, this work has provided a detailed overview of the formation and properties of AgNPs/CDs hybrid systems, paving the way for further research into the potential applications of these materials, particularly in the biomedical field, where their resistance to oxidation could be particularly beneficial.

Giorgio Giuseppe Carbone: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Daniela Manno: Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Antonio Serra: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Alessandro Buccolieri: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Sabrina Cennamo: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Muhammad Rizwan Aziz: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Alessandra Gabriele: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Lucio Calcagnile: Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the Biopen Lab of the “E. De Giorgi” mathematics and physics department of the University of Salento for the use of the Jeol JEM-ARM 200F Holo-TEM.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.L. Ventola, The Antibiotic Resistance Crisis, Pharm. Ther. 40 (2015) 277–283. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4378521/(accessed February 26, 2024).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibiotic use in agriculture and its consequential resistance in environmental sources: potential public health implications
Mol. J. Synth. Chem. Nat. Prod. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Manyi-Loh, S. Mamphweli, E. Meyer, A. Okoh, Antibiotic Use in Agriculture and Its Consequential Resistance in Environmental Sources: potential Public Health Implications, Mol. J. Synth. Chem. Nat. Prod. Chem. 23 (2018) 795. https://doi.org/10.3390/molecules23040795.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Towards the sustainable discovery and development of new antibiotics
